description: A potter expects the clay to crack before they can name every cue. With 'hiru', a person reaches that judgement or expectation without consciously following the reasoning steps. 'kethira' (infer) derives a conclusion from premises; 'sano' (know) states knowledge. An intuition may be accurate or mistaken.
articulatory_notes: Let unvoiced air pass through the open glottis for 'h' and continue into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Make 'r' with a tap or brief trill at the alveolar ridge. The word ends as the back of the tongue rises and the lips round for high-back 'u'.
sound_symbolism: Open glottal breath moves quickly into stressed 'i', then a tongue tap or short trill releases into rounded 'u'. The brief change may recall an impression arriving before its reasons are laid out.
usage_notes: An object or a proposition in 'mena ... meno' states the intuited content. The noun refers to one intuition considered as an event. 'thuni' (intuitive) supplies the corresponding quality.

examples.phi: mia mena shia so shua meno hiru.
examples.translation: I intuit that they will come.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: An intuition may be noticed as a mental event before anyone treats it as knowledge. Evidence can then test what arrived.

semantic_domains.cognition: a judgement or expectation arising without consciously followed reasoning
semantic_domains.wisdom: immediate judgement that may draw on accumulated experience before explanation
